claim: As of mid-April 2026, no Member State has fully completed nationwide transposition of Directive (EU) 2023/970 on pay transparency, with the 7 June 2026 deadline imminent.
source_title: European Union: Transposition of the EU Pay Transparency Directive Across 27 Member States (April 2026 update)

claim: The European Commission confirmed in December 2025 that the 7 June 2026 transposition deadline stays put, with no postponements granted.
source_title: EOR & Compliance Digest, April 27: Global Hiring Rule Changes in UK, Canada, EU and Germany

claim: Several Member States — including Germany, Spain, Romania and Belgium — are expected to publish further draft texts in the coming weeks, with several Member States (at least ten) facing potential infringement proceedings for failure to transpose on time.
source_title: European Union: Transposition of the EU Pay Transparency Directive Across 27 Member States

claim: On 15 April 2026, Slovakia's National Council adopted the Equal Pay Act, becoming the first EU Member State to transpose the EU Pay Transparency Directive into national law; the Act enters into force on 7 June 2026, the exact date of the EU deadline.
source_title: First past the post: Slovakia becomes the first EU Member State to transpose the Pay Transparency Directive

claim: Slovak employers must have compliant pay structures in place by 31 July 2026, just over seven weeks after the law takes effect, and same-sex equal pay claims are within scope (extending equal pay protections beyond male–female comparisons).
source_title: First past the post: Slovakia becomes the first EU Member State to transpose the Pay Transparency Directive

claim: Under Slovakia's Equal Pay Act, the first reporting window for employers with 150 or more employees only covers the period from 1 August 2026 to 31 December 2026 (rather than a full calendar year), with reports due 7 June 2027.
source_title: First past the post: Slovakia becomes the first EU Member State to transpose the Pay Transparency Directive

claim: Slovakia's Equal Pay Act sets a fine range of €4,000 to €8,000 for reporting failures, supplemented by broader sanctioning powers granted to the Labour Inspectorate via amendments to the Labour Inspection Act.
source_title: First past the post: Slovakia becomes the first EU Member State to transpose the Pay Transparency Directive

claim: Spain opened a public consultation on the draft Royal Decree implementing the Pay Transparency Directive that runs from 24 April to 8 May 2026, ahead of formal drafting of the Royal Decree.
source_title: Spain moves to strengthen pay transparency and implement EU Gender Pay Transparency Directive

claim: The planned Spanish Royal Decree would broaden workers' rights to information and impose stricter duties on employers in areas including job advertising, pay-setting policies, career progression and pay audits, but it is not yet certain transposition will be complete by 7 June 2026.
source_title: Spain moves to strengthen pay transparency and implement EU Gender Pay Transparency Directive

claim: In the week of 13 April 2026, in the Committee on Social Affairs, Employment and Pensions, Belgian Minister Clarinval confirmed Belgium's intention to fully transpose the EU Pay Transparency Directive into Belgian law by 7 June 2026; a draft has not yet been submitted but is in preparation.
source_title: Belgium Pay Transparency Reporting Law Guide

claim: As of April 2026, Belgium's federal private-sector draft is 'expected to circulate shortly', with the country expected to enter June 2026 with regional public-sector laws in force in Flanders and the Wallonia–Brussels Federation but no federal private-sector framework.
source_title: EU Pay Transparency Directive Tracker: Status by Country

claim: On 22 April 2026, a Mason Hayes & Curran survey of 500 Irish employers found that more than one in five view the incoming pay transparency rules as an 'unnecessary burden', and only 31% expect a positive effect.
source_title: One in five employers see pay transparency rule as 'unnecessary burden'

claim: The Irish Government has confirmed it will not have full compliance with the Pay Transparency Directive in place by 7 June 2026 and is expected to greenlight a phased introduction starting with pre-employment obligations including salary disclosure in job advertisements.
source_title: Employers running out of time as changes on pay transparency and retirement loom

claim: Ireland is pursuing a two-bill strategy: the Equality and Family Leaves (Miscellaneous Provisions) Bill 2024 covers the salary history ban (Article 5), while a standalone Pay Transparency Bill is still in the Heads-of-Bill drafting stage.
source_title: EU Pay Transparency Directive Tracker: Status by Country

claim: The public consultation on the Czech draft Pay Transparency Act, published by the Ministry of Labour and Social Affairs on 26 March 2026, closed on 27 April 2026.
source_title: Analyzing Czechia's Draft Legislation to Transpose the EU Pay Transparency Directive

claim: The Czech draft sets a 1 January 2027 general entry-into-force date, but most substantive obligations — including Right to Information, pay reporting and joint pay assessments — will not take effect until 1 January 2028, with certain provisions entering into force in 2031; the Czech Republic does not intend to meet the 7 June 2026 deadline.
source_title: Analyzing Czechia's Draft Legislation to Transpose the EU Pay Transparency Directive

claim: The Czech draft requires every employer to establish and document a remuneration system (via internal regulation or collective agreement) classifying jobs into groups graded by value of work, and creates a dual-report structure (a Ministry-prepared headline pay-gap report and an employer-prepared report on pay gaps within job groups due 30 April annually) that goes beyond the Directive's single reporting obligation.
source_title: Czech Republic publishes draft Pay Transparency Act: key deviations and practical issues for employers

claim: On 16 April 2026, Estonia's Economic Affairs Minister announced the government would rather pay European Commission infringement fines than transpose the Directive in its current form, citing prohibitive compliance costs.
source_title: EU Pay Transparency Directive Tracker: Status by Country

claim: Estonia's position has subsequently been clarified: the country still intends to prohibit salary history questions and provide applicants with salary range information prior to interview, even as it resists the broader Directive in its current form.
source_title: First past the post: Slovakia becomes the first EU Member State to transpose the Pay Transparency Directive

claim: Germany has not yet tabled a formal government bill; the Expert Commission's final report (published 7 November 2025) recommends a 'low-bureaucracy' implementation, with a draft bill expected in early 2026 but not yet officially announced.
source_title: EU Pay Transparency Directive — country-by-country status

claim: France's preliminary draft transposition bill, sent to social partners on 6 March 2026, is expected to be presented to Parliament in late May 2026 and is not expected to be enacted before the 7 June 2026 deadline; parliamentary debate is anticipated by the end of 2026.
source_title: France has disclosed a draft bill to implement the Gender Pay Transparency Directive

claim: France's draft bill goes well beyond the Directive's minimum requirements: salary ranges mandatory in job postings, lower reporting thresholds (50+ employees), administrative fines of up to 1% of payroll (2% for repeat offences), and an expanded role for works councils.
source_title: France Pay Transparency Draft Law: Key differences from the EU Directive

claim: Sweden reversed course on 26 March 2026, announcing the Directive is too administratively burdensome, that it wishes to postpone the implementation deadline, seeks renegotiation at EU level, and does not currently intend to submit a transposition bill to the Riksdag.
source_title: European Union: Transposition of the EU Pay Transparency Directive Across 27 Member States

claim: The Dutch government submitted a revised draft bill to the Council of State (Raad van State) on 19 January 2026, confirming entry into force of 1 January 2027 for transparency obligations and a first pay gap report by 7 June 2028 (covering calendar-year 2027 data) for employers with 150 or more employees.
source_title: EU Pay Transparency Directive — country-by-country status

claim: Denmark's draft bill, published 26 February 2026 with consultation closing 27 March 2026, is expected to enter into force on 1 January 2027, meaning Denmark will miss the 7 June 2026 deadline.
source_title: How Countries Are Implementing the EU Pay Transparency Directive (April 2026 update)

claim: Following the public consultation that closed on 8 April 2026, Romania's draft pay transparency law has moved into discussions with trade unions before parliamentary submission; there is a reasonable prospect that Romania will meet the 7 June 2026 deadline.
source_title: EU Pay Transparency Directive: complete legislation guide and FAQs (April 2026 update)

claim: A Ministry of Labour and Social Policy working group has prepared a draft amendment to the Anti-Discrimination Act and the Labour Code transposing Directive 2023/970, with adoption by the National Assembly targeted by 29 May 2026; no public draft text has yet been released, making the May 29 target ambitious.
source_title: EU Pay Transparency Directive Tracker: Status by Country

claim: Latvia's Ministry of Welfare published a draft law transposing Directive 2023/970 for public consultation that closed 9 April 2026; timely transposition by 7 June 2026 is possible but requires urgent parliamentary status.
source_title: EU Pay Transparency Directive Tracker: Status by Country
